schools.summary: 'Myriads' pressing so they trod on one another; the dispute from the Pharisee's house (Luke 11:53) drew the crowd; the warning spoken to disciples first but in the people's hearing.
Theophylact: the Pharisees' design to draw the people away is reversed — truth is mighty, deceit feeble.
Tertullian (anti-Marcion): the hypocrisy was loving God with lips not heart; the Son hates those who refuse the Father obedience.
Better 'but' than 'for' (Ellicott): whether they beware or not, all secret things will be manifested; 'covered up' (completely concealed); parallel to Matt 10:26.
Gill: nothing more covered than hypocrisy, yet it shall be detected — if not in this world, at the last judgment.

themes: nothing covered shall not be revealed

schools.summary: Proclamation 'upon the housetops' an Eastern custom; the bearing differs from Matt 10:27 (there a charge to publish, here a warning of disclosure); the 'inner chambers' the secret store-rooms.

themes: spoken in darkness heard in light

schools.summary: Ambrose: lest terror force denial of the God owned in the heart; Cyril: crowns prepared for the persecuted, the body's death the end of their suffering; Bede: the persecutors' rage is useless raving.
'My friends' set against the Pharisees and lawyers; ingressive aorist 'do not become afraid'; man's malice stops at the body.
Gill: 'my friends' — those Christ dearly loved and died for (John 15:14); Wesley: let no fear of man make you act the hypocrite.
Calvin: fearing men we disrespect God; fearing God we have easy victory over men — the emphatic doubled command.
Clarke/Wesley: this fear is to be pressed even on true believers and the most holy — madness to lose the soul to save the life.
Robertson notes the deliberative subjunctive and the doubled command's force.

themes: fear him who casts into hell
the fear of God cures the fear of man

schools.summary: The market-detail: five sparrows for two farthings (vs Matt 10:29's two for a farthing), the odd bird thrown in; yet not one forgotten before God.
Wesley: 'trust as well as fear him' — the God to be feared as Judge is the Father who forgets not a sparrow.
The hairs 'numbered' (perfect, a settled state); the argument from the lesser (sparrows) to the greater — 'ye are of more value.'
Clarke: want of faith in God's providence is the source of all our fears; man distrusts God by judging him by himself.
Gill: even the minutest parts — the very hairs — noted by the wonderful numberer.
Chrysostom: not inward faith only but outward confession required; Cyril cites Rom 10:9 — the whole mystery of Christ; Eusebius marvels at the Word bearing witness for us at the judgment.
Ambrose: faith the foundation, confession its fruit; Bede: the disclosure before the heavenly city and the eternal Judge.
Parallel to Matt 10:32; the variation 'before the angels of God' (Matthew: 'before my Father').
Gill: it means one who lives and dies a denier — a real Christian may deny under temptation and repent, as Peter; Lightfoot links it to apostasy and the sin against the Spirit.
The stronger compound 'shall be denied' (vs Matthew's simple form); 'before the angels of God.'
Ambrose: no comparison of greater/less where there is unity of power; the sin against the Spirit set in the frame of the Beelzebub charge.
Robertson/Ellicott: originally tied to the Beelzebub charge (Matt 12:31), repeated by Luke in its general bearing.
Lightfoot distinguishes apostasy and the blasphemy; Wesley: a degree of denial may be forgiven on repentance, but the blasphemy never.

debates.question: What is the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit?

sides.position: falling back into sin after baptismal grace (the ancients, via Athanasius)
the wilful, malicious rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ (the Beelzebub charge)

themes: word against the Son of man forgiven

schools.summary: The special promise to the Twelve (Matt 10:18) extended to all his friends; 'be not anxious' (do not become anxious), as on the Galilean tour and Olivet (Mark 13:11); the Lukan 'authorities and powers.'
Cyril: the martyrs bear witness by the Holy Ghost — impossible to testify for Christ except by the Spirit.
Clarke/Wesley: be not solicitous about the matter or manner of your defence.
Robertson: the Spirit teaches in the crisis-hour — no excuse for neglecting ordinary preparation, but courage to play the man and trust the issue with God.
Gill: the Spirit gives both words and matter, instantly, in the same hour.
Cyril: the martyr testifies by the power of the Holy Ghost, taught in that very hour.

themes: the Holy Ghost shall teach you

schools.summary: Ambrose: he avoids earthly strifes, having descended for divine things; Bede: he uses the foolish petitioner to fortify all against covetousness.
The law gave two-thirds to the elder (Deut 21:17); the man wants a decision against his brother, not arbitration; scribes served as property arbitrators.
Calvin: Christ declines the dispute to keep to his spiritual calling, not be entangled in the secular.
Christ refuses the secular office (echoing Exod 2:14), rendering Caesar's things to Caesar — his kingdom not of this world (John 18:36); 'divider' (meristes) only here; the grave 'Man' a censure.
Clarke: a minister of Christ should keep free of secular affairs — the example of disengagement needful for the ministry.

themes: the kingdom not of this world

schools.summary: Beware of 'all' covetousness (pleonexia, the lust for more) — a breach of the tenth commandment (Exod 20:17), idolatry (Col 3:5); Christ read the man's heart.
Calvin: covetousness flows from the false belief that happiness is produced by riches; Christ cures it by denying the premise.

Basil: the harvest a mercy of God to the bad, given that he might give to the needy — 'why do you abound while another begs?'; Cyril: his folly in saying 'my fruits' as if his own labour, not God's gift.
Calvin: the parable a lively portrait of the universal but unbelieved sentiment that life lies in possessions.
His self-enclosed cogitation (imperfect), all 'my' — like Nabal whose name means fool (1 Sam 25:11); riches engross the thoughts and steal the heart.
Calvin: the wicked are perplexed because they know not the lawful use of things, intoxicated with a confidence that forgets themselves; Wesley: 'What shall I do?' is the language of want.
The plan to pull down and build greater barns; the drumbeat of 'my'; the rich full of designs for this life, taking no thought of eternity.
Gill: the resolution reached without consulting God or asking his leave.
The Epicurean/atheist creed 'eat, drink, and be merry' (cf 1 Cor 15:32); the soul feeding on goods; the eager asyndeton of commands.
Calvin: he swells with pride, forgetting he is a man, holding his riches as a brazen rampart against death.

themes: riches as a rampart against death

schools.summary: 'Fool' (aphron, lacking sense); the impersonal plural 'they require thy soul'; in the very night his plans were settled, his soul summoned, his goods left to another.
Calvin/Wesley: the life he thought his own was at another's disposal — the messengers of death, commissioned by God, require his soul.
The ruin in 'for himself'; 'rich toward God' = rich in good works (1 Tim 6:18), the only wealth that lasts (cf Luke 16:9; Matt 6:19).
Calvin: 'rich toward God' is the opposite of laying up for self — wealth laid up with God, which death cannot touch.

Theophylact: Christ goes from covetousness to anxiety, plucking out its root; Chrysostom: 'take no thought' = not 'do no work' but 'have not your minds fixed on earthly things' — the worker may be free of anxiety, the corroding care (not labour) forbidden.

debates.question: Does 'take no thought' forbid work and provision?

sides.position: No — it forbids anxious care, not honest labour or reasonable provision

themes: take no thought for your life

schools.summary: Gill: Luke affirms what Matthew questions — life and body the greater gifts (food and raiment only their support); the God who gave life will give what sustains it, the greater gift the pledge of the lesser.

themes: the Giver of life sustains it

schools.summary: Bede: man more precious, a rational creature of higher order; the birds cited following the OT (bee, ant); Ambrose: providence feeds the toil-less birds, our poverty springing from covetousness; Cyril: the birds conveying firm trust as before.
Luke's 'ravens' (Matthew's 'birds') — the unclean, improvident bird thought to neglect its young, yet fed by God, proving providence reaches the least.

Wesley: 'add a cubit' a proverb for the least addition; whether of bodily height or the span of life, anxiety cannot accomplish even this small thing — worry proved futile, life's measure in God's hand.

debates.question: Does 'add a cubit to his stature' mean height or length of life?

sides.position: Bodily stature (a cubit a large addition)

themes: add a cubit to his stature

schools.summary: Ellicott (the verse peculiar to Luke): if anxiety cannot add a cubit (the least), how much less can it control the myriad larger contingencies of the future — worry over the greatest the more clearly vain; Robertson: 'least' an elative, 'very small.'

themes: not able to do the least
why take thought for the rest

schools.summary: Chrysostom: as with the birds, 'they toil not, neither spin' condemns not work but anxiety about it; the lily chosen (not swan or peacock) for force from both the meanness of the thing and the excellence of its God-given glory beyond Solomon's.

themes: they toil not, they spin not

schools.summary: Gill: the lilies but grass, frail and short-lived, clothed by the Creator; how much more his enduring children.

Chrysostom: 'God SO clothe' (with surpassing glory) and 'how much more you' mark his high valuing of his people; the rebuke gentle — 'little faith,' not unbelief — to rouse, not crush; anxiety diagnosed as want of faith.

themes: how much more will he clothe you

schools.summary: Clarke/Robertson: 'be not of doubtful mind' (meteōrizesthe, from 'in midair') = anxious suspense, the mind tossed and agitated; Gill: not lawful seeking of daily bread by proper means, but the tormenting care forbidden — come down from the restless height to settled trust.

themes: seek not what ye shall eat

schools.summary: Gill/Lightfoot: 'the nations of the world' = the Gentiles, who know not God; anxious seeking heathenish and unbecoming Christ's disciples; Clarke: 'seek after' = eagerly coveting one thing after another, the heathen's life regardless of eternity; the Father knows our needs — the tenderest motive against anxiety.

themes: the nations of the world seek after

schools.summary: Seek the kingdom (the rule of grace and the glory to come, Gill) in place of anxious care; Luke's shorter form omits Matthew's 'first/righteousness/all' (Robertson); 'all these things added' assures the Father's provision.

themes: seek ye the kingdom of God
all these things shall be added

schools.summary: The 'little flock' little in number, esteem, and humility, outnumbered by the reprobate and angels (Theophylact, Cyril); yet the Father gives even this little flock the kingdom of heaven.
Calvin: 'good pleasure' (eudokein) conveys free undeserved mercy — the kingdom given because it pleased the Father, not earned by works.
'Little flock' (the article, Ellicott); not a diminutive but pathetic (Robertson, against Clarke's 'little little flock'); the kingdom of righteousness, peace, and joy.
Almsgiving blots out sin and is the path to the kingdom (Chrysostom); yet not a command that saints keep no money (Christ himself had a bag, Bede) — God not to be obeyed for earthly things' sake.

A special direction to the disciples/Seventy, not a universal rule (Wesley); not Ebionism (Robertson, the literal attempt impoverished the Jerusalem church); the principle of sitting loose to possessions abides (Ellicott); treasure in heaven imperishable.

debates.question: Is 'sell that ye have' a universal binding rule?

sides.position: A special direction to the disciples/Seventy and a principle of detachment for all
Almsgiving is essential — without it none sees the kingdom

themes: sell that ye have, give alms

schools.summary: The heart inevitably follows the treasure — where the wealth is, the affections dwell (Gill); the ground of laying up heavenly treasure; a universal principle (Clarke's Plautus parallel).
Eusebius/Theophylact: the soul dwells on the object of its desire; to fix the heart on buried treasure is to sink the soul, a divine work, to the earth.
The girded loins figure chaste, active diligence (continence controlling the flesh, Gregory); the burning lamps good works and discernment shining before others (Theophylact, Gregory).
Girding the loins the Eastern symbol of readiness for active service (Barnes, Ellicott); the lamps held by the watchful; the condensed germ of the Ten Virgins (Robertson).

Christ went to the wedding at his ascension, uniting himself to the angels and the souls of the saints (Gregory, Theophylact); his return 'from the wedding' is his coming at the end or at each one's death.
Calvin: though Christ is absent in heaven, the believer must not sink into indolent repose but hold himself ready at every moment to receive his returning Lord.

The Lord himself girds and serves the watchful servants — the exceeding condescension of Christ (Philippians 2:7, Barnes), foreshadowed in the foot-washing (John 13:4, Ellicott, Robertson) and the heavenly supper (Revelation 3:20).
The 'sitting down' the saints' complete rest, the serving the abundant outpouring of gifts and joy in the kingdom (Theophylact, Dionysius).

themes: he shall gird himself and serve them

schools.summary: The second and third watches the dead of night (Lightfoot), the Jewish threefold division (Ellicott); the servant found watching even then is blessed — constant, persevering readiness.
Gregory: the watches the ages of life (childhood, youth, age) — he who neglects to watch in youth must at least watch in age, that he be found ready.

The thief illustration reinforces the waiting servants (Robertson), stressing the unexpectedness of the Lord's coming ('as a thief in the night,' 1 Thessalonians 5:2); had the householder known the hour he would have watched — so the unknown hour grounds constant vigilance.
The thief figure signifies suddenness, not stealth (Theophylact); death comes unexpectedly while the spirit sleeps (Gregory).

Be ready always — actually, in grace and duty, with loins girt (Gill); 'keep on becoming ready' (present imperative, Robertson); the unknown hour the ground of perpetual preparedness, for the Lord's coming in judgment, at death, and at the last day.

themes: at an hour when ye think not

schools.summary: Peter asks 'us or all?'; the steward-figure especially the priests/ministers (Ambrose), who need faithfulness and wisdom lest they destroy souls (Theophylact).
Henry: 'What I say unto you, I say unto all' (Mark 13:37) — watchfulness for all, yet the apostles primarily concerned; we must each take to ourselves what Christ designs for us.
Christ answers indirectly by a parable; Peter (and each of the Twelve, anyone) is the wise steward (Robertson).

themes: speakest thou this parable unto us, or to all

schools.summary: The faithful and wise steward (the minister) set over God's household to give each his food in due season; faithfulness keeps the word pure, wisdom divides it rightly (Gill); a house manager entrusted, not owning (Robertson).
Henry: the steward gives each his portion, 'to every one his own,' rightly dividing the word of truth (2 Timothy 2:15) in due season, approving himself faithful and wise.

themes: portion of meat in due season

schools.summary: The faithful steward found 'so doing' — discharging his trust with integrity (Gill) — is blessed; Wesley warns this same servant might yet fall (v.45).
Basil/Isidore: 'so doing' (not by chance but as commanded), giving the food in due season — the faithful, seasonable discharge of duty.
The solemn 'of a truth' (Christ's asseveration, Gill) guarantees the faithful steward's advancement — made ruler over all (cf. Matthew 24:47, Robertson).
Bede/Cyril: the advancement is the fuller enjoyment of the kingdom's joys — the faithful steward reckoned among the Lord's friends, possessing all abundantly.

themes: of a truth I say unto you
ruler over all that he hath

schools.summary: The evil servant holds the same office but betrays it (Gill); presuming 'My lord delayeth' (Robertson), he falls to abusing his fellow-servants and indulgence — the root of his fall the presumption of delay.
Bede: the condemnation of all evil rulers who, losing the fear of God, give themselves to pleasure and oppress those under them.

themes: eat and drink and be drunken

schools.summary: The lord comes suddenly and 'cuts in sunder' the abuser, appointing his portion 'with the unfaithful' (the untrustworthy, contrasted with the faithful steward; 'the hypocrites' in Matthew — Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson).
'Cut in sunder' = the eternal severing of soul from the Spirit (Basil), or separation from the communion of the faithful (Bede).

themes: come in a day he looketh not

schools.summary: Greater knowledge the occasion of greater punishment (Chrysostom); the knowing servant's dignity aggravates his guilt (Theophylact) — the priest sinning suffers more than the people.
Calvin: knowingly despising the Lord deserves severer punishment; the plea of ignorance is no adequate shield; privilege increases responsibility (Barnes).
The Jewish forty stripes (Deuteronomy 25:3, Lightfoot, Clarke); vv.47-50 peculiar to Luke, with a latent reference to Judas (Ellicott).

The ignorant beaten with few stripes — proportioned equity (Ellicott), a 'gleam of hope' (Augustine's tolerabilior damnatio), whether lighter or shorter left to God; 'much given, much required' — responsibility proportioned to privilege and trust.
Calvin: the more entrusted, the more required — the field cultivated at greater expense must yield more, or the abuse of grace costs dear.
Basil: the stripes mark degrees, not the end, of punishment (against universalism); the ignorant not wholly excused, ignorance often culpable (Theophylact).

debates.question: Do the 'few stripes' imply a limited/temporary punishment?

sides.position: They mark degrees of judgment, not a limit or end (against universalism)
A gleam of hope — whether lighter or shorter suffering, left to God's equity

schools.summary: The 'fire' variously the gospel, the zeal/division it provokes, persecution, the Spirit, or the fire of judgment that purifies and destroys (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott); 'what will I if it be already kindled' = 'how I wish it were kindled' (Lightfoot, Robertson).
Calvin: the gospel is fire because it violently changes the face of things; the commotions a ground of confidence, not fear; one of Christ's latest discourses, out of place.

debates.question: What is the 'fire' Christ came to send?

sides.position: The gospel/its transforming and dividing power
The fire of judgment that purifies and destroys
Persecution, zeal, or the Holy Spirit

themes: I am come to send fire
what will I if it be already kindled

schools.summary: The 'baptism' Christ's passion and death — an immersion in suffering, bathed in blood (Gill, Robertson, Ellicott); 'how am I straitened' the intense constraint of the coming Cross on his human soul, dreaded and longed for (Robertson, Barnes).
Calvin: the baptism the consecrating death by which Christ renews the world; he is straitened, pressing eagerly toward the Cross.

themes: I have a baptism to be baptized with

schools.summary: Christ brings spiritual peace but not outward peace (Gill); peace at any price is not his purpose — loyalty to Christ counts more than family harmony (Robertson).

Calvin: the prophets' peace belongs to the godly; to unbelievers, who cannot endure reconciliation, the message excites greater tumult — Christ, properly the author of peace, becomes the occasion of division through human wickedness.

themes: peace to the godly, tumult to unbelievers

schools.summary: The gospel divides the household itself — five split, three against two — as some receive Christ and others reject him (Gill); the cause the enmity between the Spirit of Christ and the spirit of the world (Wesley).

The division reaches every household relation — parent and child, mother-in-law and daughter-in-law (Gill, echoing Micah 7:6); those who reject Christ implacable toward their nearest who receive him, fulfilled 'at this day' (Wesley).

The western cloud foretells a shower (Barnes); the people read the sky accurately yet are blind to the signs of the Messiah's coming — natural shrewdness against spiritual blindness, a failure of will not ability.

themes: a cloud rise out of the west

schools.summary: The south wind from the hot regions (the scorching simoom, Ellicott) surely brings heat, as the western cloud brings rain; the people's exact weather-lore makes their blindness to the signs of the time inexcusable (Barnes).

Christ rebukes the people as 'hypocrites' for willful blindness — able to read the sky but refusing to discern 'this time,' the season of the Messiah and their visitation (Ellicott, Luke 19:44); the failure of will, not ability (Robertson).

themes: discern the face of the sky

schools.summary: Christ appeals to conscience and reason — they had light enough to judge rightly of the time and of Christ (Romans 1-3, Robertson); the fault not lack of knowledge but failure to act on it (Ellicott).
Calvin: the hypocrites do not descend into their consciences to examine before God what is right, but throw out objections without calm thought or submission to truth.

themes: why even of yourselves judge ye not

schools.summary: Literally, prudence in settling lawsuits before the magistrate (Matthew 5:25, Clarke); allegorically, the adversary the Law that accuses, the judge the Judge of all (Ellicott) — urging reconciliation before judgment.

The adversary read as the devil, an evil conscience, every vice, or the word of God opposing carnal desire (Ambrose, Bede, Origen); the urgency of reconciling the soul with God before judgment (drawn from the visible, Chrysostom).

themes: as thou art in the way

schools.summary: No release till the last mite (Luke's smallest coin, Ellicott) is paid — the rigour and completeness of the reckoning; the inexorable justice awaiting the unreconciled.
Clarke: suffering can never pay the debt to divine justice; the urgency of present reconciliation, since none can plead ignorance who has God's word at hand.
The 'last mite' read as graduated payment (Origen) or the endless prison where penalty is ever paid but pardon never obtained (Bede).

debates.question: Does 'till the last mite' imply a temporary, payable punishment?

sides.position: Endless judgment — the penalty ever paid but pardon never obtained
Graduated payment by degrees of debt
Suffering can never pay the debt — the urgency of present reconciliation

themes: till thou hast paid the last mite
